elements.sent-2411-001: We report measurements of the mean settling velocities for suspensions of discs and rods in the stokes regime for a number of particle aspect ratios.
elements.sent-2411-002: All these shapes display ''hindered settling'', namely, a decrease in settling speed as the solid volume fraction is increased.
elements.sent-2411-003: A comparison of our data to spheres reveals that discs and rods show less hindering than spheres at the same relative interparticle distance.
elements.sent-2411-004: The data for all six of our particle shapes may be scaled to collapse on that of spheres, with a scaling factor that depends only on the volume of the particle relative to a sphere.
elements.sent-2411-005: Despite the orientational degrees of freedom available with nonspherical particles, it thus appears that the dominant contribution to the hindered settling emerges from terms that are simply proportional to the volume of the sedimenting particles.
elements.sent-2411-006: Sedimentation, the gravitational settling of solid particles in a liquid, presents a complex challenge in classical many-body physics, particularly in the highly-viscous, overdamped Stokes regime where particle velocity fields decay very slowly with distance $r$ from the particle, as $1/r$.
elements.sent-2411-007: Many puzzles have emerged in studies of the sedimentation of suspensions of spheres\cite{chaikin2000thermodynamics,caflisch1985variance,nicolai1995particle,xue1992diffusion,mucha2004model , especially on the scaling of fluctuations with system size.}
elements.sent-2411-008: In this article we address a basic question on the mean sedimentation rate in the collective settling of suspensions of nonspherical particles, which are far more common in natural and technological sediments such as clays, snowflakes, ice crystal, minerals, phytoplankton, reaction precipitates or paper fibres.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{Fig_1.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_1} Data for discs and rods are compared to those of spheres held at the same normalized separation $a^*=a/L$, where $a$ is the mean interparticle separation and $L$ is the largest particle dimension, i.e. diameter $2c$ of hex and discs or the length $b$ of rods.} \captionof{table}{\label{tab:table1} Particle geometry, measured Stokes velocity, Reynolds number and P\'eclet number } \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{lccccc} Aspect ratio, shape c (mm) b (mm) $V_S$ (mm/s) Re Pe \colrule A=0.15 hex 0.27$\pm$0.01 0.08$\pm$0.001 0.04$\pm$0.001 $6.33\times 10^{-5}$ $1.07\times 10^{10}$ A=0.07 hex 0.65$\pm$0.02 0.09$\pm$0.003 0.13$\pm$0.003 $4.96\times 10^{-4}$ $1.36\times 10^{11}$ A=0.05 disc 0.66$\pm$0.003 0.06$\pm$0.002 0.10$\pm$0.004 $3.88\times 10^{-4}$ $1.32\times 10^{11}$ A=19 rod 0.15$\pm$0.0003 5.69$\pm$0.012 0.36$\pm$0.004 $4.25\times 10^{-4}$ $6.65\times 10^{12}$ A=10 rod 0.26$\pm$0.0009 5.45$\pm$0.030 0.42$\pm$0.004 $2.37\times 10^{-4}$ $2.07\times 10^{13}$ A=3 rod 0.49$\pm$0.0004 2.97$\pm$0.015 0.89$\pm$0.001 $2.74\times 10^{-4}$ $4.34\times 10^{13}$ \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-009: The question we wish to address is how the settling velocity $V_S$ of an isolated particle of typical size $\ell$ in a fluid of viscosity $\eta$ and density $\rho$ is modified by the presence of a finite volume fraction $\phi$ of other particles.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our focus is on the Stokes regime where the Reynolds number $Re=\rho V_S \ell/\eta\ll1$.
elements.sent-2411-011: This effect is captured by the "hindered settling function" $H(\phi)$, a dimensionless ratio defined as $H=V/V_S$, where $V=V(\phi)$ is the mean sedimentation velocity of particles in suspension and $V_S$ is the stokes velocity of an isolated particle.
elements.sent-2411-012: As the name implies, in suspensions of spheres, settling is hindered, with a monotonic decrease from $H(0)=1$ as volume fraction $\phi$ is increased\cite{davis1985sedimentation,ramaswamy2001issues .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Brzinski and Durian\cite{brzinski2018observation made an extensive compilation of data for sphere-sedimentation from previous studies.}
elements.sent-2411-014: These studies include data for both small $Pe$ (brownian) and large $Pe$ (non-brownian), where the P\'eclet number $Pe=V_S\ell/D$ with diffusion coefficient $D$, quantifies the relative efficacy of convective to diffusive transport.
elements.sent-2411-015: These data are typically fit by the empirical Richardson-Zaki form $H(\phi)=(1-\phi)^n$\cite{richardson1997sedimentation with best-fit values of $n\approx 4.5$ for the large $Pe$, non-brownian regime that we consider in this article.}
elements.sent-2411-016: There is no theory of $H(\phi)$ over the entire range of volume fractions; indeed, the only rigorous hydrodynamic calculation owes to Batchelor\cite{batchelor1972sedimentation who predicted that in the dilute limit, $\phi\rightarrow{0}$, $H(\phi)=1-6.55\phi+\mathcal{O (\phi^2)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Shape leads to a qualitative difference in single particle dynamics and pair interactions: while a single sphere falls straight down without rotation, rods, discs, and ellipsoids drift horizontally and have vertical settling speeds that depend on their orientation relative to gravity\cite{taylor1969motion,batchelor1970slender,mackaplow1996study,mackaplow1998numerical,happel2012low .}
elements.sent-2411-018: A pair of spheres falls faster than one sphere, remaining at fixed separation\cite{happel1960motion , however pairs of discs settling at low Reynolds number display either bound periodic orbits or scattering states\cite{chajwa2019kepler,jung2006periodic due to the coupling between orientational and translational degrees of freedom.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Likewise, a sedimenting lattice of disc shows very different collective behaviour\cite{chajwa2020waves than a lattice of spheres\cite{crowley1971viscosity .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the central question of this article is whether the effects of orientational degrees of freedom lead to a significant difference in collective behaviour of a finite volume fraction -- as opposed to a finite number -- of shaped particles compared to sphere suspensions, where many more studies exist.
elements.sent-2411-021: A prominent example of previous work is the sedimentation of rod-like particles studied in a series of papers by Guazzelli and coworkers\cite{herzhaft1996experimental,herzhaft1999experimental,guazzelli2011fluctuations,metzger2005large,metzger2007experimental , who identified two distinct regimes of sedimentation for fibers: at higher volume fractions, they observe hindered settling reminiscent of sphere-suspensions, whereas at low enough volume fractions where the fibres can rotate freely without steric hindrance, sedimentation leads to a complex pattern of instabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We are not aware of comparable studies for the geophysically relevant case of flat particles at finite volume fraction.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this article, we present new data on the hindered settling of flat shapes (discs and hexagons) and elongated shapes (rods) in the Stokes regime, as a function of both particle aspect ratio and solid volume fraction, while remaining in a semi-dilute regime, as discussed later.
elements.sent-2411-024: These shapes are nonpolar (i.e. head-tail symmetric) and effectively axisymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-025: Despite the wide range of aspect ratio of particles that we explored, our principal observation is that the dominant contribution to the hindered function $H(\phi)$ emerges from terms proportional to the volume of a single particle.
elements.sent-2411-026: We report results for three different aspect ratios each of flat (discs, hexagons) and long shapes (rods).
elements.sent-2411-027: All particle dimensions are given in Table~\ref{tab:table1 with the radius $c$, thickness/length $b$, and aspect ratio $A=b/2c$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The error bars, measured by the standard error on a small population of individual particles, shows that the discs, hex, and especially the rods, are very monodisperse.
elements.sent-2411-029: The rods were stainless steel, and used after cleaning and demagnetization in a degaussing coil.
elements.sent-2411-030: The discs and hex particles were polyimide, and used as purchased.
elements.sent-2411-031: The particles are suspended in silicone oil of three different viscosities, $\eta=350, 5000$ or $10000$ $cSt$, chosen to produce a Reynolds number $Re$ of approximately $10^{-5}$ to $ 10^{-4}$ in all situations.
elements.sent-2411-032: To obtain a known volume fraction $\phi$, we first measured the density of the solid particles using a pycnometer (EISCO Labs).
elements.sent-2411-033: We first filled the pycnometer of $10\pm0.03 ml$ with distilled water to ascertain its precise volume.
elements.sent-2411-034: We then added a known mass of particles and a few drops of 1\% soap solution which suppressed bubbles from adhering to solid surfaces, then filled the container with distilled water.
elements.sent-2411-035: Using the measured density of the fluid, we were able to calculate the density of the particles.
elements.sent-2411-036: The precise volume of the sedimentation cell is then measured, and a sample of known volume fraction $\phi$ can be prepared with a precision of $\pm 3\%$.
elements.sent-2411-037: We placed partially filled sample containers and a beaker of silicone oil in a vacuum chamber for more than 24 hours to remove bubbles.
elements.sent-2411-038: The sample containers were then nearly fully filled with silicone oil from the beaker.
elements.sent-2411-039: We applied vacuum again for several hours to remove the remaining bubbles and then overfilled the samples with silicone oil.
elements.sent-2411-040: Next, we sealed the bottles with Teflon tape and threaded lids.
elements.sent-2411-041: Once a sample was prepared, we put them in a home-built tube rotator for mixing overnight at low frequency (a few r.p.m.) to generate a well-mixed homogeneous state at the chosen volume fraction $\phi$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.15\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2a.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.15\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2b.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.66\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2c.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth] {Fig_2d.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_2}(a) Sample of $\phi=0.04, A=0.15$ hex particles imaged with scattered light. (b) Sample of $\phi=0.18, A=3$ rods imaged with transmitted light. The rectangles show the region selected for analysis. (c) Grey value of all selected images from (a), as a function of height at a series of times. The grey value is the intensity averaged over the horizontal direction. (d) Image sequence for the sample in (a). The dots are the center points of interfaces found by analyzing the gradient of curves in (c). Green dots mark the interface detected between the suspension and the supernatant fluid. The yellow dots mark the interface between the suspension and the sediment solid. The red and purple lines are fits to the two sets of interface positions, whose slopes represent the sedimentation velocity $V$ and the velocity $V_p$ of the advancing solid precipitate, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The specimens are then quickly placed vertically with a precision of $\pm 0.2 \degree$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The settling process was observed by taking images with a Nikon DSLR camera of the entire cell at fixed time intervals of typically $5 s$ until all solid particles have settled at the bottom.
elements.sent-2411-044: For white particles (the hex and discs) we illuminated the sample from the side and observed light scattered by particles into the camera, whereas for the opaque rods, we chose back lighting to produce a high contrast, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (a)\&(b).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{subfigure}{0.8\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth] {Fig_3a.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_3b.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_3c.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_3}(a) Hindered settling function $H$ increases as normalized interparticle separation $a^*$ increases for all particles we measured. Inset: The blue and orange curves are the Richardson-Zaki form $H=(1-0.18(a^{*})^{-3})^n$, with n=4.5 \& 5.5 respectively\cite{richardson1997sedimentation}, which represent fits to sphere sedimentation. (b) $H(a^*)$ data shifted by a factor $S(A)$ to collapse on the R-Z curve with $n=4.5$ based on the total least square method\cite{golub1973some,golub1980analysis}. We also include data from Herzhaft \& Guazzelli and Turney et al. \cite{herzhaft1999experimental,turney1995hindered}. (c) Shift factor $S(A)$ versus the logarithm of the aspect ratio $A$ for both flat and long particles. The red dot for spheres is by definition at $S=1, A=1$. We also show a black square to indicate a cylinder with $A=1$, placed at the appropriate volume relative to the sphere. }
elements.sent-2411-045: To obtain $V(\phi)$, the sedimentation velocity, we adopted a method based on that of Ref.~\cite{brzinski2018observation .}
elements.sent-2411-046: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (a)\&(b), we select a central rectangular region, and identify the position of the interface by taking gradients of the smoothed, horizontally-averaged intensity profile shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (c).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: To show the outcome of this process, we display in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (d) a time-series of eighteen images stitched together.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The upper green dots are the identified location of the interfaces between the suspension and the supernatant fluid.
elements.sent-2411-049: The upper red line is a fit to the green dots that represent the top interface.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is well-fit by a straight line, implying that the sedimentation velocity $V(\phi)$ is constant over the entire settling process, and that the mean sedimentation is a steady state process.
elements.sent-2411-051: This in turn implies that the sample was well mixed initially and reproducibly prepared.
elements.sent-2411-052: In a few cases, it is hard to detect the interface of suspension and the supernatant fluid when scattering or absorption of the illumination by the particle does not form a sharply-defined interface.
elements.sent-2411-053: Here we used the OpenPIV package for Particle Image Velocimetry to detect the velocity of individual particles and averaged over many particles to obtain the sedimentation velocity $V$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In addition to the velocity $V(\phi)$ with which the top interface recedes, for the flat particles which we image by scattered light, we also obtain the velocity $V_p$ with which the solid precipitate front grows, as shown by the lower set of yellow symbols and the purple line in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (d).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Mass conservation of the solid implies $V\phi + V_p(\phi-\phi_p)= 0$, which allows us to extract the volume fraction $\phi_p$ of the precipitate\cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-056: All the sedimentation velocity data are tabulated in the Supplemental Material\cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-057: To obtain the hindered function $H(\phi)$ from $V$, we normalize by the stokes velocity $V_S$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We determined the stokes velocity from measurements of a single particle dropped in a container much larger than the particle size.
elements.sent-2411-059: A complication is that $V_S$ for an anisotropic particle depends on orientation.
elements.sent-2411-060: We measured $V_S$ for vertically oriented rods, but the hex and discs were too small to fix an initial orientation, and the data averages over initial orientations.
elements.sent-2411-061: All the data are shown in Table~\ref{tab:table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Finite size effects arising from the wall are expected to be smaller for the sedimentation velocity $V$ itself, due to screening of the wall effect in the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-063: We confirm this expectation for a series of $\phi=0.04, A=0.15$ hex samples in containers with varying heights and widths as shown in the Supplemental Material\cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-064: In Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (a) we show the principal experimental results of this article.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Rather than as a function of $\phi$, we plot the hindered settling function as a function of $a^*=a/L$, namely, the interparticle separation, $a$, normalized by the largest dimension $L$ of the particle i.e. the diameter of hex and discs, and length of rods (see Fig. \ref{fig:Fig_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-066: The interparticle separation, $a$ is computed from the experimental volume fraction $\phi$ assuming a random spatial distribution of particles\cite{hertz1909gegenseitigen,chandrasekhar1943stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-067: That is, $a^*=a/L=k \phi^{-1/3}$, where $k=0.447$ for spheres, and $k$ depends on aspect ratio $A$ for rods and discs as specified in the Supplemental Material\cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-068: The data shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (a) indicate a monotonically increasing trend for $H(a^*)$, showing that the effect of increasing volume fraction is indeed to hinder collective sedimentation, as is the case with spheres.}
elements.sent-2411-069: As a point of reference, we show in the inset curves for the Richardson-Zaki function with $n=4.5$ and $5.5$ (which represent global fits\cite{brzinski2018observation to data for spheres at $Pe>10^8$ and $Pe<10^8$, respectively, in this regime of volume fraction).}
elements.sent-2411-070: By comparison, deviations from $H=1$ appear at smaller interparticle separation $a^*$ for discs and rods compared to spheres.
elements.sent-2411-071: Indeed, for these shaped particles, hindering appears for $a^*<1$, or $a<L$, in a regime where the particles cannot rotate freely.
elements.sent-2411-072: Said differently, with the same relative spatial distribution, non-spherical particles show much less hindering than spheres indicating that spheres occupy a significantly higher hydrodynamic volume than nonspherical particles.
elements.sent-2411-073: To more quantitatively compare between different aspect ratio, $A$, we shifted the data for each shape by a factor $S(A)$ to achieve the best possible collapse against spheres, by minimizing the total least squared\cite{golub1973some,golub1980analysis deviation between the data and the Richardson-Zaki function with $n=4.5$, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-074: We also included data from Herzhaft \& Guazzelli and Turney et al. \cite{herzhaft1999experimental,turney1995hindered in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (b), shifted in the same manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: To the extent that these eight data sets collapse within experimental error, the shift factor $S(A)$ captures the shape-dependence of the hindering effect of equally spaced particles with equal largest dimension.
elements.sent-2411-076: To explore further this shape dependence, we plot in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (c) the shift factor $S(A)$ against the logarithm of the aspect ratio $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: When scaled to the largest particle dimension $L$, these aspect ratio dependencies capture the relative volume of these shapes, with the volume of sphere, disc and rod expressed as $\pi/6, \pi A/4$ and $\pi /(4A^2)$ in units of $L^3$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Thus the scaling of the shift factor $S(A)$ as $A^{-1}$ for discs and $A^{2}$ for rods is exactly the ratio of volume of the sphere to the volume of the shape.
elements.sent-2411-079: We note that the point for spheres, set at $S(A)=1, A=1$, lies above the intersection of the rod and the disc scaling.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is to be expected, as the volume of the inscribed sphere is less than the volume of the cylinder with $A=1$ that should lie at the intersection point.
elements.sent-2411-081: The data thus point to the significance of particle volume in hindering.
elements.sent-2411-082: We return to discuss the dominant contributors to hindering first.
elements.sent-2411-083: As mentioned earlier, for spheres in the dilute limit ($\phi\rightarrow{0}$), Batchelor found $H(\phi)=1-6.55\phi+\mathcal{O (\phi^2)$\cite{batchelor1972sedimentation .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this calculation, the dominant contribution to the hindering is from backflow.
elements.sent-2411-085: When particles settle, they must displace fluid upwards equal to their volume.
elements.sent-2411-086: In addition, a settling particle also drags the surrounding fluid down, which scales with the sphere volume.
elements.sent-2411-087: This is irrelevant for a single particle as the return flow is at infinity, but for a finite volume fraction, the corresponding volume of upward backflow goes through the bulk of the suspension.
elements.sent-2411-088: These two contributions amount to -5.5$\phi$ out of the -6.55$\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Only the remainder comes from hydrodynamics interactions that go beyond the mean flow: -1.55$\phi$ and +0.5$\phi$ come from the probability of close encounters among spheres and the hydrodynamic interactions via the second derivative of the velocity field generated by other spheres\cite{batchelor1972sedimentation, chaikin2000thermodynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-090: We are not aware of detailed calculations beyond the dilute regime for the relative contributions of backflow (both from the solid and the entrained fluid) versus interparticle hydrodynamic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-091: For the same relative spatial distribution and number density, $\phi$ is determined decided by the solid volume of individual particles.
elements.sent-2411-092: Then the backflow which dominates the hindering is proportional to the volume of the particle.
elements.sent-2411-093: This explains why the shift factor $S(A)$ for discs and rods is captured by the ratio of their solid volume to that of a sphere.
elements.sent-2411-094: Our data therefore suggest that the mean hindered settling of discs and rods in the stokes regime are to a first degree not more complicated than that of spheres, despite the orientational degrees of freedom that potentially add new physics to the sedimentation of shaped particles.
elements.sent-2411-095: Subtler effects such as the width of the sedimentation interface or fluctuations that could be introduced by the orientational degrees of freedom remain to be explored in systems of shaped particles.
elements.sent-2411-096: We also note that our conclusions are formed based on experiments in the semi-dilute regime where interparticle separation is of the order of the particle size.
elements.sent-2411-097: At lower volume fractions, rods, at least, have been shown to experience segregation into dense lanes of streamers and particle-depleted regions where the backflow can bypass particle-rich regions.
elements.sent-2411-098: These instabilities appear to be system-size dependent and the boundary between the stable, hindered, region and the unstable regions deserves further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-099: Of course, as one enters higher density regimes, translational and orientational degrees of freedom can become constrained due to both steric and hydrodynamic interactions and then more complicated physics can emerge.

elements.sent-2411-103: Rodrigues for her help with density measurements on discs.
elements.sent-2411-104: The radius of the rod is measured with a micrometer($\pm0.001mm$), and its length with an optical microscope ($\pm0.01mm$) calibrated to a reticle.
elements.sent-2411-105: For discs, the radius is also measured using the microscope.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thickness measurements are conducted with a contact profilometer, specifically the KLA Tencor Alpha D-500.
elements.sent-2411-107: We first lie the particle on a flat and smooth glass substrate.
elements.sent-2411-108: A stylus moves in a straight line along the surface of the substrate and then over the particles surface, just making contact.
elements.sent-2411-109: The jump in height of the stylus over the particles is used for the thickness of the particle.
elements.sent-2411-110: We repeated measurements ten times along different paths for different particles and took the average value yielding our final result of thickness with a precision of $\pm0.001mm$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Standard deviations of these measurements were used to estimate the monodispersity of the particle sizes reported in the main text.
elements.section-2411-003: \textit {S2. Stokes velocity formula of disc and rod}
elements.sent-2411-112: The horizontal and vertical settling velocity components, $V_x$ and $V_z$, of a circular disc\cite{happel2012low , \begin{subequations \begin{equation}V_{x}=\frac{\pi cbg\Delta\rho}{64\eta}sin2\theta\label{subeq:1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}V_{z}=\frac{\pi cbg\Delta\rho}{64\eta}(5+cos2\theta)\label{subeq:2}\end{equation} \end{subequations The settling velocity components of a rod\cite{batchelor1970slender,mackaplow1996study,mackaplow1998numerical , \begin{subequations \begin{equation}V_{x}=\frac{c^2g\Delta\rho}{4\eta}(ln2A-1.807+\mathcal{O}(ln2A)^{-1})sin\theta cos\theta\label{subeq:1}\end{equation} \begin{equation}V_{z}=\frac{c^2g\Delta\rho}{4\eta}[ln2A+0.193+\mathcal{O}(ln2A)^{-1}+(ln2A-1.807+\mathcal{O}(ln2A)^{-1})cos^2\theta]\label{subeq:2}\end{equation} \end{subequations For both rods and discs, $c$ is the radius, and $b$ is the thickness of disc and the length of rod, $\Delta\rho$ is the density difference between the particle and fluid, $\eta$ is fluid viscosity, $A$ is the aspect ratio, $\theta$ is the particle orientation relative to the gravity, that is $\theta=0$ for vertical and $\theta=\pi/2$ for horizontal orientations of the particle axis.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Normalized interparticle separation $a^*$ of discs and rods, \begin{equation}a^*_{hex}=0.48(\frac{A}{\phi})^{1/3}\end{equation} \begin{equation}a^*_{disc}=0.511(\frac{A}{\phi})^{1/3}\end{equation} \begin{equation}a^*_{rod}=0.511(A^2\phi)^{-1/3}\end{equation} A is the aspect ratio and $\phi$ is the volume fraction.
elements.sent-2411-114: For most of our data, we directly studied the interface between the suspension and the supernatant as described in the main paper.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, for the lowest volume fraction of rod suspensions ($\phi=0.02$), we used a PIV-based analysis as the interface was difficult to identify.
elements.sent-2411-116: We captured images of the sedimentation process at a 1s interval.
elements.sent-2411-117: We then cropped a $2cm\times2cm$ central region containing $30-40$ rods.
elements.sent-2411-118: For each successive image, the crop window was moved downward by a fixed distance (25 or 30 pixels) corresponding to a estimated settling velocity, allowing us to track the same group particles over time.
elements.sent-2411-119: We used the OpenPIV package for Particle Image Velocimetry to detect the velocities of individual particles.
elements.sent-2411-120: The typical window size for the PIV search is a little larger than the length of the rod.
elements.sent-2411-121: By averaging these velocities across many particles over different times and trials, we measured the sedimentation velocity $V$.
elements.section-2411-006: \textit {S5. Summary of all the mean sedimentation velocity data}
elements.sent-2411-122: A summary of all the mean sedimentation velocity data are given in the Table~\ref{tab:table3 .}
elements.sent-2411-123: The error in $\phi$ is calculated by the propagation of the error in measurements of particle density and container volume.
elements.sent-2411-124: The error in $V$ comes from the error in slope of the linear fit of the red line in Fig. 2(c) in the main paper.
elements.sent-2411-125: The error in $V_S$ is found by the standard error of multiple measurements.
elements.sent-2411-126: The error in hindered settling function $H$ is derived from the error propagation of $V$ and $V_S$.
elements.sent-2411-127: The volume fraction $\phi_p$ of the disc precipitate, calculated based on the mass conservation of the solid volume: $V\phi + V_p(\phi-\phi_p)= 0$, is shown in Table~\ref{tab:table2 .}
elements.sent-2411-128: The error in $V_p$ comes from the error in slope of the linear fit of the purple line in Fig. 2(c) in the main paper.
elements.sent-2411-129: The error in $\phi_p$ is calculated by the error propagation of $\phi$, $V$ and $V_p$.
elements.sent-2411-130: Both for the rods, which were imaged under backlighting rather than scattered light, and for the higher volume fractions of discs, there was insufficient contrast to identify the interface between the precipitate and the suspension.
elements.sent-2411-131: Thus we were not able to obtain equivalent measurements of $V_p$ (and hence, $\phi_p$) for all our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-132: To assess the effect of finite container size has on the sedimentation velocity, we made a series of $\phi=0.04, A=0.15$ hex samples in containers with varying heights and widths, and with rectangular and circular cross-sections.
elements.sent-2411-133: As shown in the Table~\ref{tab:table1 the stokes velocity $V_S$ increases as the system width is increased.}
elements.sent-2411-134: However, the sedimentation velocity $V$ is nearly unaffected by system size.
elements.sent-2411-135: This indicates that our experimental systems are large enough to measure a bulk value of the settling velocity of a suspension at these volume fraction $\phi$.

elements.sent-2411-136: A comparison of our data to spheres reveals that discs and rods show less hindering than spheres at the same relative interparticle separation.
elements.sent-2411-137: Despite the orientational degrees of freedom available with nonspherical particles, it thus appears that the dominant contribution to the hindered settling emerges from terms that are simply proportional to the volume of the sedimenting particles, enabling prediction of hindered settling of other nonpolar axisymmetric shapes.
elements.sent-2411-138: Many puzzles have emerged in studies of the sedimentation of suspensions of spheres \cite{chaikin2000thermodynamics,caflisch1985variance,nicolai1995particle,xue1992diffusion,mucha2004model , especially on the scaling of fluctuations with system size.}
elements.sent-2411-139: In this article, we address a basic question on the mean sedimentation rate in the collective settling of suspensions of nonspherical particles, which are far more common in natural and technological sediments, such as clays, snowflakes, ice crystal, minerals, phytoplankton, reaction precipitates or paper fibres.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{Fig_1.jpg} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_1} Data for discs and rods are compared to those of spheres held at the same normalized interparticle separation $a^*=a/L$, where $a$ is the mean interparticle separation and $L$ is the largest particle dimension, i.e. diameter $2c$ of discs or the length $b$ of rods. Though the hex particle is not axisymmetric, its single-particle dynamics is the same as a disc; $L=2c$ is chosen to be the diameter of the circumcircle.} \captionof{table}{\label{tab:table1} Particle geometry, measured Stokes velocity, Reynolds number and P\'eclet number } \begin{ruledtabular} \begin{tabular}{lccccc} Aspect ratio, shape c (mm) b (mm) $U_S$ (mm/s) Re Pe \colrule A=0.15 hex 0.27$\pm$0.01 0.08$\pm$0.001 0.04$\pm$0.001 $6.33\times 10^{-5}$ $1.07\times 10^{10}$ A=0.07 hex 0.65$\pm$0.02 0.09$\pm$0.003 0.13$\pm$0.003 $4.96\times 10^{-4}$ $1.36\times 10^{11}$ A=0.05 disc 0.66$\pm$0.003 0.06$\pm$0.002 0.10$\pm$0.004 $3.88\times 10^{-4}$ $1.32\times 10^{11}$ A=19 rod 0.15$\pm$0.0003 5.69$\pm$0.012 0.36$\pm$0.004 $4.25\times 10^{-4}$ $6.65\times 10^{12}$ A=10 rod 0.26$\pm$0.0009 5.45$\pm$0.030 0.42$\pm$0.004 $2.37\times 10^{-4}$ $2.07\times 10^{13}$ A=3 rod 0.49$\pm$0.0004 2.97$\pm$0.015 0.89$\pm$0.001 $2.74\times 10^{-4}$ $4.34\times 10^{13}$ \end{tabular} \end{ruledtabular}
elements.sent-2411-140: The question we address is how the settling velocity $U_S$ of an isolated particle of typical size $\ell$ in a fluid of dynamic viscosity $\eta$ and density $\rho$ is modified by the presence of a finite volume fraction $\phi$ of other particles.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our focus is on the Stokes regime where the Reynolds number $Re=\rho U_S \ell/\eta\ll1$.
elements.sent-2411-142: This effect is captured by the "hindered settling function" $H(\phi)$, a dimensionless ratio defined as $H=U/U_S$, where $U=U(\phi)$ is the mean sedimentation velocity of particles in suspension and $U_S$ is the stokes velocity of an isolated particle.
elements.sent-2411-143: As the name implies, in suspensions of spheres, settling is hindered, with a monotonic decrease from $H(0)=1$ as volume fraction $\phi$ is increased \cite{davis1985sedimentation,ramaswamy2001issues .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Brzinski and Durian \cite{brzinski2018observation made an extensive compilation of data for sphere-sedimentation from previous studies.}
elements.sent-2411-145: These studies include data for both small $Pe$ (brownian) and large $Pe$ (non-brownian), where the P\'eclet number $Pe=U_S\ell/D$ with diffusion coefficient $D$, quantifies the relative efficacy of convective to diffusive transport.
elements.sent-2411-146: These data are typically fit by the empirical Richardson-Zaki form $H(\phi)=(1-\phi)^n$ \cite{richardson1997sedimentation with best-fit values of $n\approx 4.5$ for the large $Pe$, non-brownian regime that we consider in this article.}
elements.sent-2411-147: There is no theory of $H(\phi)$ over the entire range of volume fractions; indeed, the only rigorous hydrodynamic calculation owes to Batchelor \cite{batchelor1972sedimentation who predicted that in the dilute limit, $\phi\rightarrow{0}$, $H(\phi)=1-6.55\phi+\mathcal{O (\phi^2)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Shape leads to a qualitative difference in single particle dynamics and pair interactions: while a single sphere falls straight down without rotation, rods, discs, and ellipsoids drift horizontally and have vertical settling speeds that depend on their orientation relative to gravity \cite{taylor1969motion,batchelor1970slender,mackaplow1996study,mackaplow1998numerical,happel2012low .}
elements.sent-2411-149: A pair of spheres falls faster than one sphere, remaining at fixed separation \cite{happel1960motion , however pairs of discs settling at low $Re$ display either bound periodic orbits or scattering states \cite{chajwa2019kepler,jung2006periodic due to the coupling between orientational and translational degrees of freedom.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Likewise, a sedimenting lattice of discs shows very different collective behaviour \cite{chajwa2020waves than a lattice of spheres \cite{crowley1971viscosity .}}
elements.sent-2411-151: A prominent example of previous work on rods comes from Guazzelli and coworkers \cite{herzhaft1996experimental,herzhaft1999experimental,guazzelli2011fluctuations,metzger2005large,metzger2007experimental , who identified two distinct regimes of sedimentation for fibers: at very low volume fractions they found sedimentation is accompanied by a complex pattern of instabilities, whereas at higher volume fraction, they observed steady hindered settling reminiscent of sphere-suspensions.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In this Letter, we present new data on the hindered settling of flat shapes (discs and hexagons) and elongated shapes (rods) in the Stokes regime, as a function of both particle aspect ratio and solid volume fraction, while remaining in a semi-dilute regime.
elements.sent-2411-153: Our principal observation is that the dominant contribution to the hindered function $H(\phi)$ emerges from terms proportional to the volume of a single particle, over the wide range of aspect ratios that we explored.
elements.sent-2411-154: All particle dimensions are given in Table~\ref{tab:table1 with the radius $c$, thickness/length $b$, and aspect ratio $A=b/(2c)$ \cite{supp .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The error bars, measured by the standard error on a population of individual particles, show that the discs, hex, and especially the rods, are very monodisperse.
elements.sent-2411-156: The particles are suspended in silicone oil of density $0.97 g/cm^3$ and various kinematic viscosities ($350, 5000$ or $10000$ $cSt$) chosen to produce Reynolds number $Re$ of approximately $10^{-5}$ to $ 10^{-4}$ in all situations.
elements.sent-2411-157: We prepare samples in a known volume fraction $\phi$ and initialize them to be spatially homogeneous as described in the End Matter.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{subfigure}{0.15\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2a.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.15\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2b.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.66\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_2c.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth] {Fig_2d.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_2}(a) Sample of $\phi=0.04, A=0.15$ hex particles imaged with scattered light. (b) Sample of $\phi=0.18, A=3$ rods imaged with transmitted light. The rectangles show the region selected for analysis. (c) Grey value of all selected images from (a), as a function of height at a series of times. The grey value is the intensity averaged over the horizontal direction. (d) Image sequence for the sample in (a). The dots are the center points of interfaces found by analyzing the gradient of curves in (c). Green dots mark the interface detected between the suspension and the supernatant fluid. The yellow dots mark the interface between the suspension and the sediment solid. The red and purple lines are fits to the two sets of interface positions, whose slopes represent the sedimentation velocity $U$ and the velocity $U_p$ of the advancing solid precipitate, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-158: The specimens are quickly placed vertically with a precision of $\pm 0.2 \degree$.
elements.sent-2411-159: During sedimentation we took images with a Nikon DSLR camera of the entire cell at time intervals of typically $5 s$ until all solid particles settled at the bottom.
elements.sent-2411-160: For white particles (the hex and discs) we illuminated the sample from the side and imaged the scattered light, whereas for the opaque rods, we chose back-lighting to produce a high contrast, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (a)\&(b).}
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{subfigure}{0.8\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth] {Fig_3a.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_3b.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=5cm, width=\linewidth, keepaspectratio] {Fig_3c.jpg} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{\label{fig:Fig_3}(a) Hindered settling function $H$ increases as normalized interparticle separation $a^*(\phi)$ increases for all particles we measured. Inset: The blue and orange curves are the Richardson-Zaki form $H=(1-\phi(a^*))^n$ with exponents n=4.5 \& 5.5 respectively \cite{richardson1997sedimentation}, which represent fits to sphere sedimentation. (b) $H(a^*)$ data shifted by a factor $S(A)$ to collapse on the R-Z curve with $n=4.5$ based on the total least square method \cite{golub1973some,golub1980analysis}. We also include data from Herzhaft \& Guazzelli and Turney et al. \cite{herzhaft1999experimental,turney1995hindered}. (c) Shift factor $S(A)$ versus the logarithm of the aspect ratio $A$ for both flat and long particles. The red dot for spheres is by definition at $S=1, A=1$. We also show a black square to indicate a cylinder with $A=1$, placed at the appropriate volume relative to the sphere. }
elements.sent-2411-161: To obtain $U(\phi)$, the sedimentation velocity, we adapted a method based on Ref.~\cite{brzinski2018observation .}
elements.sent-2411-162: The outcome of this analysis is displayed in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (d) where a time-series of images is stitched together.}
elements.sent-2411-163: The top interface is well-fit by a straight line (red line), implying that the sedimentation velocity $U(\phi)$ is constant over the entire settling process, and that the mean sedimentation is a steady-state process.
elements.sent-2411-164: This in turn implies that the sample was well-mixed initially and reproducibly prepared.
elements.sent-2411-165: For very low $\phi$, it is hard to detect the interface of between the suspension and the supernatant fluid when scattering or absorption of the illumination does not yield a sharply-defined interface.
elements.sent-2411-166: Here we used Particle Image Velocimetry \cite{supp to detect the velocity of individual particles and averaged over many particles to obtain the sedimentation velocity $U$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: All sedimentation data are tabulated in the Supplemental Material \cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-168: In the End Matter, we discuss finite-size effects on $U$, and measurements on the growth velocity of the suspension-precipitate interface (yellow symbols in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (d)).}
elements.sent-2411-169: To obtain the hindered function $H(\phi)$ from $U$, we normalize by the stokes velocity $U_S$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We determined the stokes velocity from measurements of individual particles dropped in a container much larger than the particle size.
elements.sent-2411-171: A complication is that $U_S$ for an anisotropic particle depends on orientation \cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-172: We measured $U_S$ for vertically oriented rods, but the flat particles were too small to fix an initial orientation, and the data averages over initial orientations.
elements.sent-2411-173: Rather than as a function of $\phi$, we plot the hindered settling function as a function of $a^*=a/L$, namely, the mean interparticle separation, $a$, normalized by the largest dimension $L$ of the particle, i.e., the diameter of hex and discs, and length of rods (see Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_1 ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: The interparticle separation, $a$ is computed from the experimental volume fraction $\phi$ assuming a random spatial distribution of particle centres: $a^*=a/L=k \phi^{-1/3}$, where $k$ depends on aspect ratio $A$ \cite{hertz1909gegenseitigen,chandrasekhar1943stochastic,vroege1992phase,eppenga1984monte,veerman1992phase as specified in the Supplemental Material \cite{supp .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: The data shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (a) indicate a monotonically increasing trend for $H(a^*)$, showing that the effect of increasing volume fraction is indeed to hinder collective sedimentation, as with spheres.}
elements.sent-2411-176: As a point of reference, we show in the inset curves for the Richardson-Zaki function with $n=4.5$ and $5.5$ which represent global fits \cite{brzinski2018observation to data for spheres at $Pe>10^8$ and $Pe<10^8$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Indeed, for shaped particles, hindering manifests for $a^*<1$, or $a<L$, in a semi-dilute regime where particles cannot rotate freely.
elements.sent-2411-178: Said differently, with the same relative spatial distribution, non-spherical particles show much less hindering than spheres.
elements.sent-2411-179: To more quantitatively compare between different aspect ratios, $A$, we shifted the data horizontally for each shape by a factor $S(A)$ to achieve the best possible collapse against spheres, by minimizing the total least squared deviation \cite{golub1973some,golub1980analysis between the shifted data and the Richardson-Zaki function with $n=4.5$, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Fig_3 (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-180: We also included data from Refs.
elements.sent-2411-181: Thus the scaling of the shift factor $S(A)$ as $A^{-1}$ for discs and $A^{2}$ for rods is exactly the ratio of the sphere volume to the volume of the shape.
elements.sent-2411-182: This is as expected, since the volume of the inscribed sphere is less than the volume of the cylinder with $A=1$ that should lie at the intersection point.
elements.sent-2411-183: The data thus point to the significance of particle volume.
elements.sent-2411-184: A clue to the dominant contributors to hindering are available in the exact calculation by Batchelor for spheres in the dilute limit ($\phi\rightarrow{0}$), where he found $H(\phi)=1-6.55\phi+\mathcal{O (\phi^2)$ \cite{batchelor1972sedimentation .}}
elements.sent-2411-185: This calculation identifies backflow as the dominant contribution to the hindering.
elements.sent-2411-186: When particles settle, there must be fluid displaced upwards equal to their volume.
elements.sent-2411-187: In addition, a settling particle also drags surrounding fluid down, which scales with the sphere volume.
elements.sent-2411-188: The upward flux of fluid is irrelevant for the stokes velocity of an isolated particle as the return flow is at infinity, but for a finite $\phi$, the upward backflow goes through the bulk of the suspension which amounts to a contribution of -5.5$\phi$ out of -6.55$\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-189: The remainder comes from hydrodynamic interactions that go beyond the mean flow: -1.55$\phi$ and +0.5$\phi$ come from the probability of close encounters between spheres and hydrodynamic interactions via the second derivative of the velocity field generated by other spheres \cite{batchelor1972sedimentation, chaikin2000thermodynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-190: We are not aware of any other Batchelor-style calculations in the dilute limit for anisotropic particles, nor are there calculations beyond the dilute regime for the relative contributions of backflow versus interparticle hydrodynamic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-191: However, the combined data from spheres, discs and rods suggests that backflow dominates even at finite $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-192: Our data therefore suggest that the mean hindered settling of discs and rods in the stokes regime are to a first degree not more complicated than that of spheres, despite the orientational degrees of freedom that potentially add new physics to sedimentation dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-193: This observation enables prediction of the hydrodynamic contribution to hindering of any nonpolar axisymmetric shape, such as a paper fibre, a red blood cell, or a clay platelet.
elements.sent-2411-194: Subtler effects such as the sharpness of the sedimentation interface or fluctuations introduced by orientational degrees of freedom remain to be explored.
elements.sent-2411-195: Our conclusions are based on experiments in the semi-dilute regime where interparticle separation is of the order of the particle size.
elements.sent-2411-196: At lower volume fractions, rods have been shown to experience segregation into dense lanes of streamers and particle-depleted regions where the backflow can bypass particle-rich regions \cite{metzger2007experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-197: These instabilities appear to be system-size dependent and the boundary between the stable, hindered regime we studied and the unstable regime deserves further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-198: At higher volume fractions, translational and orientational degrees of freedom can become constrained due to both steric and hydrodynamic interactions and more complicated physics can emerge.
elements.sent-2411-200: Determining volume fraction To obtain a suspension of a known volume fraction $\phi$, we first measured the mass density of the solid particles using a pycnometer (EISCO Labs).
elements.sent-2411-201: We filled the pycnometer of $10\pm0.03 ml$ with distilled water to ascertain its precise volume.
elements.sent-2411-202: Using the measured density of the fluid, we calculated the mass density $\rho_s$ of the particles.
elements.sent-2411-203: The precise volume of the sedimentation cell $V$ is then measured, and a mass $m$ of particles is added.
elements.sent-2411-204: This leads to a sample of known volume fraction $\phi=m/(\rho_s V)$ with a precision of better than $\pm 2.7\%$, as described in the Supplemental Material \cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-205: The largest quantifiable source of error in $\phi$ comes from the measurement of the density $\rho_s$ of each of the types of particle.
elements.sent-2411-206: In addition, there may be additional small errors from particles adhering to surfaces, or from imperfect filling.
elements.sent-2411-207: Error in $\phi$ has a systematic effect on all volume fractions of a given type of particle.
elements.sent-2411-208: Both these errors affect the quality of the scaling in Fig. 3, whereas random errors in $U$ do not.) Sample preparation We placed partially-filled sample containers with particles in silicone oil and a beaker of silicone oil in a vacuum chamber for more than 24 hours to remove bubbles.
elements.sent-2411-209: The sample containers were then nearly filled with silicone oil from the beaker.
elements.sent-2411-210: Once a sample was prepared, we put it in a home-built tube rotator for mixing overnight at low frequency (typically at a few r.p.m.) to generate a well-mixed homogeneous initial condition at the chosen volume fraction $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-211: Finite size effects Finite-size effects arising from the wall are expected to be smaller for the sedimentation velocity $U$ itself, due to screening of wall effects in the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-212: We confirm this expectation for a series of $\phi=0.04, A=0.15$ hex samples in containers with varying heights and widths as shown in the Supplemental Material \cite{supp .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Precipitate volume fraction In addition to $U(\phi)$ for the receding top interface, for the flat particles imaged by scattered light, we also obtain the velocity $U_p$ with which the solid precipitate front grows, as shown by the the yellow dots and the purple line in Fig .~\ref{fig:Fig_2 (d).}
elements.sent-2411-214: Mass conservation of the solid implies $(U+U_p)\phi=U_p\phi_p$ which allows us to extract the volume fraction $\phi_p$ of the precipitate.
elements.sent-2411-215: A consistency check is provided by directly measuring $\phi_p$ from the height of the precipitate after sedimentation is complete; the agreement of the value of $\phi_p$ from these two methods \cite{supp suggests that the measured interface velocity also tracks particle sedimentation dynamics.}
